Homework 3

Author

Katie Zhao

Text Mining

On the course Github, navigate to the page for dataset pubmed (https://github.com/dmcable/BIOSTAT620W26/tree/main/data/pubmed) and download the pubmed.csv dataset. This dataset contains 3,241 abstracts from articles collected via 5 PubMed searches. The search terms are listed in the second column, term and these will serve as the “documents.” Your job is to analyze these abstracts to find interesting insights.

  1. How many abstracts are associated with each search term? Tokenize the abstracts and count the number of each token. Do you see anything interesting? What are the 5 most common tokens for each search term?
library(tidyverse)
pubmed <- read.csv("pubmed.csv")

table(pubmed$term) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  reframe(Search_Term = Var1,
            Number_Abstracts = Freq) %>% 
  arrange(desc(Number_Abstracts)) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Number of pubmed.csv Abstracts Associated with Each Search Term")
Number of pubmed.csv Abstracts Associated with Each Search Term
Search_Term Number_Abstracts
covid 981
prostate cancer 787
preeclampsia 780
cystic fibrosis 376
meningitis 317 
## Tokenize the abstracts
library(tidytext)
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(token, abstract) %>% 
  count(token) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n)) %>% 
  top_n(10, n) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(token, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Tokens Among all 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by Appearance",
       x = "Count",
       y = "Tokens") +
  theme_bw()

As expected, among all abstracts in the pubmed.csv dataset, many of the words that made the “Top 10” list were “filler/preposition” words, such as the, of, and, in, to, a, with, and is. Of note, covid and 19 were the only two “unique” words that made this Top 10 list. Generally speaking, this Top 10 list provides no context to the text of tokens from which this list arises from; it could apply to different contexts that all use these similar filler/preposition words.

## Tokenize the abstracts
library(tidytext)
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(token, abstract) %>% 
  count(term, token) %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  arrange(n, .by_group = TRUE) %>% #technically not necessary, but groups df
  top_n(5, n) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n), .by_group = TRUE) %>% 
  reframe(Search_Term = term, Token = token, Count = n) %>% 
  select(-term) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 5 Tokens Among 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by Appearance, per Search Term")
Top 5 Tokens Among ‘pubmed.csv’ Abstracts by Appearance, per Search Term
Search_Term Token Count
covid the 9741
covid of 7668
covid covid 7275
covid 19 7035
covid and 6861
cystic fibrosis the 2677
cystic fibrosis of 2304
cystic fibrosis and 1777
cystic fibrosis in 1497
cystic fibrosis to 1011
meningitis the 2489
meningitis of 2269
meningitis and 1683
meningitis in 1322
meningitis a 830
preeclampsia the 7119
preeclampsia of 6837
preeclampsia and 5255
preeclampsia in 3869
preeclampsia to 2651
prostate cancer the 6100
prostate cancer of 5682
prostate cancer and 4417
prostate cancer cancer 3840
prostate cancer prostate 3832
  1. (15 points) Does removing stop words change what tokens appear as the most frequent? What are the 5 most common tokens for each search term after removing stop words?
# Use 'stop_words' table in tidytext

# Most frequent tokens
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(token, abstract) %>% 
  anti_join(stop_words, c("token" = "word")) %>% 
  count(token) %>% 
  top_n(10, n) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(token, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Tokens Among all 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by Appearance,\nAfter Removing 'Stop Words'",
       x = "Count",
       y = "Tokens") +
  theme_bw()

After removing the stop words from the set of tokens found in the abstract, the Top 10 Tokens by Appearance list now looks very different from what it did in the Question 1. Now, this list includes many more “domain-specific” words, such as covid, patients, cancer, prostate, etc. Before, the Top 10 list could’ve applied to text from many different contexts, but now, there is a clear sense of that the texts of these tokens originate from a medical/health sciences context.

# Most frequent tokens, by search term
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(token, abstract) %>% 
  anti_join(stop_words, c("token" = "word")) %>% 
  count(term, token) %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  top_n(5, n) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n), .by_group = TRUE) %>% 
  reframe(Search_Term = term, Token = token, Count = n) %>% 
  select(-term) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 5 Tokens Among 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by Appearance, per Search Term, After Removing Stop Words")
Top 5 Tokens Among ‘pubmed.csv’ Abstracts by Appearance, per Search Term, After Removing Stop Words
Search_Term Token Count
covid covid 7275
covid 19 7035
covid patients 2293
covid disease 943
covid pandemic 800
cystic fibrosis fibrosis 867
cystic fibrosis cystic 862
cystic fibrosis cf 625
cystic fibrosis patients 586
cystic fibrosis disease 400
meningitis patients 446
meningitis meningitis 429
meningitis meningeal 219
meningitis csf 206
meningitis clinical 187
preeclampsia pre 2038
preeclampsia eclampsia 2005
preeclampsia preeclampsia 1863
preeclampsia women 1196
preeclampsia pregnancy 969
prostate cancer cancer 3840
prostate cancer prostate 3832
prostate cancer patients 934
prostate cancer treatment 926
prostate cancer disease 652

Similarly to the Top 10 Tokens list before/after removing stop words, the Top 5 Tokens per Search Term list after removing stop words now is a lot more domain specific in context. Medical terminology, such as disease, pandemic, meningitis, patients, pregnancy, etc, are all words that were not seen on this list previously because they were buried under stop words that are commonly used to string text together.

  1. (15 points) Tokenize the abstracts into bigrams. Find the 10 most common bigrams and visualize them.
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_ngrams(ngram, abstract, 2) %>% 
  count(ngram) %>% 
  top_n(10, n) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(ngram, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Bigrams Among All 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by Appearance",
       x = "Count",
       y = "Bigram") +
  theme_bw()
  1. (15 points) Calculate the TF-IDF value for each word-search term combination (here you want the search term to be the “document”). What are the 5 tokens from each search term with the highest TF-IDF value? How are the results different from the answers you got in Question 1?
pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(token, abstract) %>% 
  count(term, token) %>% 
  bind_tf_idf(token, term, n) %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  top_n(5, tf_idf) %>% 
  arrange(desc(tf_idf), .by_group = TRUE) %>% 
  select(token, term, tf_idf) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 5 Tokens among 'pubmed.csv' Abstracts by TF_IDF Score, per Search Term")
Top 5 Tokens among ‘pubmed.csv’ Abstracts by TF_IDF Score, per Search Term
token term tf_idf
covid covid 0.0597183
pandemic covid 0.0065670
coronavirus covid 0.0053110
sars covid 0.0030536
cov covid 0.0027417
cf cystic fibrosis 0.0116541
fibrosis cystic fibrosis 0.0090127
cystic cystic fibrosis 0.0089608
cftr cystic fibrosis 0.0028167
sweat cystic fibrosis 0.0027184
meningitis meningitis 0.0147974
meningeal meningitis 0.0075539
pachymeningitis meningitis 0.0051394
csf meningitis 0.0040453
meninges meningitis 0.0036562
eclampsia preeclampsia 0.0229802
preeclampsia preeclampsia 0.0213527
pregnancy preeclampsia 0.0035250
maternal preeclampsia 0.0028993
gestational preeclampsia 0.0021891
prostate prostate cancer 0.0501967
androgen prostate cancer 0.0039953
psa prostate cancer 0.0036940
prostatectomy prostate cancer 0.0028164
castration prostate cancer 0.0019387

TD-IDF is a measure of a word’s importance to a document (abstracts of a given search term) within a larger collection (all other abstracts in dataset).

To a certain extent, this measurement helps “balance out” the frequency ranking of stop terms that commonly appear in a document, but also commonly appear in other documents. For example, in Question 1, the Top 5 tokens in abstracts associated with the covid search term include the, of, and and (stop words). Here, the 5 tokens with the highest TF_IDF scores in abstracts associated with the covid search term are covid, pandemic, coronavirus, sars, and cov—words that are more “domain-specific.” This same trend is seen among the other highest scoring TF_IDF tokens of other search terms.

Sentiment Analysis

  1. (20 points) Perform a sentiment analysis using the NRC lexicon. What is the most common sentiment for each search term? What if you remove "positive" and "negative" from the list?
library(textdata)

pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(word, abstract) %>% 
  inner_join(get_sentiments("nrc"), by = join_by(word),
             relationship = "many-to-many") %>% 
  count(term, sentiment) %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  top_n(1, n) %>% 
  select(-n) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Most Common Sentiment Associated with Each Search Term")
Most Common Sentiment Associated with Each Search Term
term sentiment
covid positive
cystic fibrosis positive
meningitis negative
preeclampsia positive
prostate cancer negative

The most common sentiments associated with each search term are “positive” and “negative.”

# Remove "positive" and "negative" assocations from sentiment
nrc_subset <- get_sentiments("nrc") %>% 
  filter(sentiment != "positive",
         sentiment != "negative")

pubmed %>% 
  unnest_tokens(word, abstract) %>% 
  inner_join(nrc_subset, by = join_by(word),
             relationship = "many-to-many") %>% 
  count(term, sentiment) %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  top_n(1, n) %>% 
  select(-n) %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Most Common Sentiment Associated with Each Search Term, Excluding 'Positive' and 'Negative'")
Most Common Sentiment Associated with Each Search Term, Excluding ‘Positive’ and ‘Negative’
term sentiment
covid fear
cystic fibrosis disgust
meningitis fear
preeclampsia anticipation
prostate cancer fear

Once “positive” and “negative” sentiments are removed from the NRC lexicon, the top sentiment seen across the covid, meningitis, and prostate cancer terms is “fear.” “Digust” and “anticipation” are the top sentiments seen for cystic fribrosis and preeclampsia, respectively.

  1. (20 points) Now perform a sentiment analysis using the AFINN lexicon to get an average positivity score for each abstract (hint: you may want to create a variable that indexes, or counts, the abstracts). Create a visualization that shows these scores grouped by search term. Are any search terms noticeably different from the others?
figa <- pubmed %>% 
  mutate(abstract_num = c(1:dim(pubmed)[1])) %>%
  relocate(abstract_num, .before = "abstract") %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, abstract) %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn")) %>%
  group_by(abstract_num, term) %>%
  summarize(mean_score_abstract = mean(value)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = mean_score_abstract)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "blue") +
  facet_wrap(~term) +
  labs(x = "Mean Positivity Score",
       y = "Count",
       title = "Figure A: Distribution of Mean\nPositivity Scores for Each Abstract,\nby Search Term") +
  theme_bw()

figb <- pubmed %>% 
  mutate(abstract_num = c(1:dim(pubmed)[1])) %>%
  relocate(abstract_num, .before = "abstract") %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, abstract) %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn")) %>%
  group_by(abstract_num, term) %>%
  summarize(mean_score_abstract = mean(value)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = term, y = mean_score_abstract)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "blue") +
  labs(x = "Mean Positivity Score",
       y = "Count",
       title = "Figure B: Distribution of Mean\n Positivity Scores for Each Abstract,\nby Search Term") +
  theme_bw() + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

cowplot::plot_grid(figa, figb)

Looking at Figure A, the “covid” and “preeclampsia” terms have a similar distribution of average positivity scores for abstracts. The distribution of scores for the abstracts corresponding to these search terms are both centered around 0 with somewhat “normal curve”-esque distributions. The “cystic fibrosis” and “meningitis” also have similar distributions. They are “flatter” and “wider” in their distributions of mean positivity scores of their abstracts, appearing to cover a larger range of scores compared to than the other terms. The “prostate cancer” search term stands out among the other distributions. While it also appears to have abstracts with a normal-curve distribution of mean positivity scores, it has a tighter distribution around 0 (smaller standard deviation) compared to the “covid” and “preeclampsia” terms.

Figure B shows further facets of the distributions of Figure A, showing that the average “mean positivity scores of abstracts” of the cystic fibrosis term is noticeably higher than the other terms. The prostate cancer search term has the smallest range of mean abstract positivity scores, and meningitis has the largest range.
